text:
CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC) AND MRI LIVER WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old female with known borderline resectable pancreatic head/uncinate adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V mutant, moderately differentiated, pT3 N0 at clinical staging). Presenting for definitive restaging following completion of 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Baseline tumor measured 3.4 cm with >180 degrees SMA abutment and SMV lumen narrowing at approximately 40%. Prior 4-cycle interim restaging (approximately 2 months ago) demonstrated a modest decrease to 3.0 cm with improvement in SMA contact to approximately 160 degrees. CA 19-9 at this visit: 85 U/mL (baseline 1,200 U/mL; prior restaging 380 U/mL).

TECHNIQUE:

CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed following administration of intravenous iohexol contrast (Omnipaque 350, 120 mL) with triphasic acquisition: non-contrast, late arterial/pancreatic parenchymal phase (approximately 40 seconds post-injection), and portal venous phase (approximately 70 seconds post-injection). Thin-section axial images were acquired at 0.625 mm collimation with multiplanar reformats in the coronal and sagittal planes. Curved planar reformatted images were generated along the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV)/portal vein axis for vascular evaluation. Maximum intensity projection (MIP) images were reviewed for vascular anatomy.

MRI Liver: Dedicated liver MRI performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet with hepatocyte-specific gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadoxetate disodium, Eovist/Primovist, 0.025 mmol/kg IV). Sequences acquired: T2-weighted fast spin echo (axial and coronal), diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI, b-values 0, 400, 800 s/mm2) with corresponding ADC maps, T1 in-phase and opposed-phase, dynamic contrast-enhanced T1-weighted fat-suppressed sequences (pre-contrast, arterial, portal venous, delayed at 3 minutes, and hepatobiliary phase at 20 minutes post-injection).

COMPARISON: CT abdomen/pelvis pancreatic protocol dated approximately 8 weeks prior (4-cycle restaging); original baseline CT at time of diagnosis.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREATIC PRIMARY TUMOR:
The hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process now measures approximately 2.6 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 3.0 cm on the 4-cycle restaging scan; 3.4 cm at baseline), representing continued interval decrease in tumor size over the course of neoadjuvant therapy. The margins of the lesion remain ill-defined and spiculated, as is typical of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma both before and after chemotherapy. The lesion is hypovascular relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma on the pancreatic parenchymal phase, which remains preserved, though the parenchymal enhancement of the uninvolved pancreatic body and tail appears mildly diminished compared to expected normal, possibly reflecting mild treatment-related or underlying chronic changes. There is mild upstream pancreatic duct prominence within the pancreatic body and tail measuring up to 4 mm, a slight improvement compared to the 5 mm noted on the baseline study, likely reflecting improved biliary/ductal drainage as the mass has decreased in size. No pancreatic duct cutoff sign is identified at the level of the mass.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC ARTERY (SMA) RELATIONSHIP:
Careful review of the thin-section axial images and curved planar reformats along the SMA demonstrates that the tumor now contacts the right lateral and posterior wall of the SMA over an arc of approximately 120 degrees of circumferential contact (previously estimated at approximately 160 degrees on the 4-cycle restaging scan, and greater than 180 degrees at baseline). There is no imaging evidence of frank encasement. The SMA wall appears smooth without intraluminal narrowing or thrombosis on MIP and multiplanar reformats. There is persistent soft tissue density/stranding immediately adjacent to the SMA in this segment, which is difficult to reliably distinguish from desmoplastic/fibrotic reaction versus residual viable tumor given the imaging characteristics of treated pancreatic adenocarcinoma. No radiographic evidence of arterial stenosis is appreciated.

SUPERIOR MESENTERIC VEIN (SMV) AND PORTAL VEIN CONFLUENCE:
The SMV demonstrates near-complete restoration of its normal luminal caliber at the level of the tumor. On the current study, there is only mild soft tissue abutment of the right lateral wall of the SMV without appreciable luminal narrowing, a meaningful improvement compared to the approximately 40% lumen compromise seen at baseline and the mildly persistent narrowing noted at the 4-cycle restaging. The portal vein confluence is well-preserved. No SMV thrombosis or occlusion. No venous collateral formation to suggest hemodynamically significant venous compromise. The portal vein and splenic vein appear normal in caliber.

CELIAC AXIS AND HEPATIC ARTERY:
The celiac axis, common hepatic artery, and proper hepatic artery maintain their normal relationships with the pancreatic mass without abutment or encasement. No encasement or narrowing of these vessels is identified. The splenic artery is patent and courses along the superior aspect of the pancreatic body without involvement.

PERIPANCREATIC AND REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
There are several small soft tissue density lymph nodes in the peripancreatic and periportal regions. The largest measures approximately 8 mm in short-axis dimension in the peripancreatic station, unchanged from prior. No lymph node demonstrates unambiguous morphologic criteria for malignant involvement (none are rounded, necrotic, or significantly enlarged beyond the threshold for concern). Given the known primary, even normal-caliber nodes cannot exclude microscopic disease, but there is no radiographically apparent nodal progression.

LIVER (CT COMPONENT):
The liver is normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on CT. The portal venous phase demonstrates homogeneous hepatic enhancement without focal areas of hypo- or hyperattenuation suspicious for metastatic disease. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The common bile duct measures approximately 7 mm in diameter (within normal limits given the known pancreatobiliary pathology), unchanged from prior.

MRI LIVER (DEDICATED LIVER EVALUATION):
No focal hepatic lesions are identified on any MRI sequence. There are no foci of restricted diffusion (DWI/ADC) within the hepatic parenchyma that would suggest metastatic deposits. The hepatobiliary phase demonstrates homogeneous uptake of gadoxetate disodium throughout the liver without focal defects. No arterially enhancing lesions. No portal venous phase washout. No hepatic capsular retraction. The liver parenchyma demonstrates normal signal intensity on T1 and T2-weighted sequences without evidence of diffuse hepatic metastatic infiltration. No intrahepatic ductal dilation.

PERITONEUM AND OMENTUM:
No peritoneal thickening, nodularity, or implants are identified. The omentum appears unremarkable. No ascites. No evidence of omental caking.

GALLBLADDER AND EXTRAHEPATIC BILE DUCT:
The gallbladder is present with a thin wall and no cholelithiasis. The common bile duct is 7 mm, as noted above, without intraluminal filling defects.

SPLEEN:
Normal in size, approximately 10 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesions.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable. No adrenal masses.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis. No suspicious renal lesions.

LUNG BASES (VISUALIZED ON CT):
The imaged lung bases demonstrate mild bibasilar dependent atelectasis. No pleural effusions. No discrete lung base nodules or masses identified on the limited lung base images included in the abdominopelvic examination. Dedicated chest imaging is not included in this examination.

BONE AND SOFT TISSUES:
No lytic or blastic bony lesions identified within the imaged skeleton. No soft tissue masses. No subcutaneous edema.

VASCULAR:
Aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilation. Iliac vessels are patent without stenosis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued interval decrease in the size of the pancreatic head/uncinate mass, now measuring approximately 2.6 cm (previously 3.0 cm at 4-cycle restaging, 3.4 cm at baseline), consistent with an ongoing partial response to neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy. This represents a reduction of approximately 24% from baseline in greatest dimension.

2. Meaningfully improved relationship between the tumor and the superior mesenteric artery. SMA circumferential contact has decreased to an estimated 120 degrees of arc (from greater than 180 degrees at baseline and approximately 160 degrees at 4-cycle restaging), though persistent abutment is noted. Continued soft tissue stranding along the SMA makes definitive margin assessment difficult by imaging alone, as treatment-related fibrosis and desmoplasia may appear morphologically indistinguishable from residual viable tumor on CT.

3. Near-complete resolution of SMV luminal compromise. The SMV now demonstrates only mild soft tissue abutment at the site of prior significant lumen narrowing, representing substantial improvement from the approximately 40% luminal compromise seen at baseline. No SMV thrombosis.

4. No hepatic metastases on dedicated liver MRI with gadoxetate disodium. The sensitivity of hepatobiliary-phase MRI for small hepatic deposits is high; the absence of lesions is reassuring.

5. No peritoneal metastases or ascites identified.

6. No radiographically apparent lymph node metastases.

7. The biochemical response is corroborated by the CA 19-9 decline from 1,200 U/mL at baseline to 85 U/mL at this restaging, representing a greater than 90% reduction -- a pattern associated with favorable treatment response.

8. Overall, these findings are consistent with a partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy with improved but persistent SMA abutment at 120 degrees. The case would benefit from review at a multidisciplinary hepatopancreaticobiliary tumor board to determine whether the degree of vascular improvement is sufficient to consider surgical exploration, or whether additional local therapy (e.g., chemoradiation) would be beneficial prior to resection.
